subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 11! In this unit you will grow into a confident and accurate calculator, able to multiply and divide large numbers using written methods that work every single time. You already know your times tables and how to share small amounts. Now you will learn how to handle much bigger numbers – the kind that appear when a warehouse in Berbera counts thousands of cans, when a trader in Eastleigh works out the cost of hundreds of items, or when Amina divides a delivery of dates fairly among many families for iftar.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 5 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, copy every worked example onto paper and try it yourself, and only move on once you feel sure. Your AI tutor is available day and night to set you extra questions, explain a step in a different way, or mark your working. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels difficult. Nobody is rushing you – accuracy matters far more than speed at this stage.

Why does this matter so much? Multiplication and division are everywhere in daily life. Shopkeepers use them to price goods and give correct change. Farmers use them to work out how many sacks of grain a harvest will fill. Families use them to share food fairly and to budget their shillings. Builders use them to count bricks and tiles. When you become fluent with these written methods, you gain a skill you will use for the rest of your life – and you build the strong foundation you need for fractions, decimals, ratio and algebra in the years ahead. Keep a pencil and paper beside you at all times, because mathematics is a subject you must do, not merely read.
Estimate the size of an answer before you calculate, by rounding.
Multiply a three-digit number by a one-digit number using short multiplication.
Multiply a three-digit number by a two-digit number using long multiplication.
Divide using short division and long division by a one-digit or two-digit number.
Express remainders as whole numbers, fractions, or decimals, choosing sensibly for the context.
Multiply and divide whole numbers and decimals by 10, 100 and 1000.
Use divisibility tests for 2, 4, 5, 8 and others, and identify prime and composite numbers.
Check every calculation using the inverse operation and a sensible estimate.

concepts.title: Estimate First, Every Time
concepts.explanation: Before you calculate anything in this unit, you will make an estimate. An estimate is a quick, sensible answer that you find by rounding the numbers to something easy, usually the nearest ten or hundred. It is not the real answer – it is a guard that tells you roughly what the real answer should be, so that if your careful working goes wrong you will notice at once. Suppose you need to work out 56 × 27. Round 56 up to 60 and round 27 up to 30. Now the sum is easy: 60 × 30 = 1800. So your final answer should be…
concepts.example: The same idea works for division. To estimate 736 ÷ 8, think of a nearby number that 8 divides easily. Since 720 ÷ 8 = 90 and 800 ÷ 8 = 100, your answer should sit between 90 and 100. When your long division gives 92,…

concepts.title: Short Multiplication (by a one-digit number)
concepts.explanation: Short multiplication is used when you multiply a larger number by a single digit. You multiply the ones, then the tens, then the hundreds, carrying across whenever a column comes to ten or more. Let us multiply 346 × 7 together, step by step. First, estimate: 346 rounds to 350, and 350 × 7 = 2450, so we expect an answer near 2400.
concepts.example: Ones: 6 × 7 = 42. Write the 2 in the ones column and carry the 4 into the tens.

concepts.title: Long Multiplication (by a two-digit number)
concepts.explanation: When you multiply by a two-digit number, you use long multiplication. The idea is simple: you split the two-digit number into its tens and its ones, multiply by each part separately to get two partial products, and then add the partial products together. Let us work out 56 × 27 in full. Estimate first: 60 × 30 = 1800, so we expect an answer close to 1800.
concepts.example: Multiply by the ones (7): 56 × 7 = 392. Write this as the first partial product.

concepts.title: Short and Long Division
concepts.explanation: Division means splitting an amount into equal groups. There are two ways to picture it. Sharing asks, ‘If I share 240 shillings equally among 6 people, how much does each get?’ Grouping asks, ‘How many groups of 6 can I make from 240?’ Both give the same answer, 40. Knowing both pictures helps you decide what a word problem is really asking. Short division (sometimes called the ‘bus stop’ method) is used when you divide by a single digit. Let us work out 736 ÷ 8. Estimate first: 720 ÷ 8 = 90, so we expect a little…
concepts.example: Hundreds: 7 ÷ 8 = 0 remainder 7. The 8 does not fit into 7, so carry the 7 across to the tens, making 73.

concepts.title: Remainders – Three Ways to Write Them
concepts.explanation: Sometimes division does not work out evenly and there is a remainder – an amount left over that is too small to make another whole group. A remainder can be written in three different ways, and part of being a good mathematician is choosing the right way for the situation. Take 97 ÷ 5. Using short division, 5 goes into 9 once with 4 left over (carry the 4 to make 47), and 5 goes into 47 nine times with 2 left over. So the answer is 19 remainder 2. Here are the three ways to record that:
concepts.example: As a whole-number remainder: 19 remainder 2 (written 19 r 2).

concepts.title: Multiplying and Dividing by 10, 100 and 1000
concepts.explanation: There is a beautiful pattern for multiplying and dividing by powers of ten, and it works because of place value. When you multiply by 10, every digit becomes worth ten times as much, so each digit moves one place to the left. When you multiply by 100, digits move two places left; by 1000, three places left. Dividing is the exact reverse: digits move to the right – one place for 10, two places for 100, three places for 1000. For whole numbers this looks like adding or removing zeros. For example, 47 × 100 = 4700…
concepts.example: Watch how it works with decimals. Start with 3.6. Multiply by 10 and each digit moves one place left, giving 36. Multiply 3.6 by 100 and the digits move two places, giving 360. Now divide: 3.6 ÷ 10 moves the digits one…

methods.title: How to do long multiplication
steps: Estimate by rounding both numbers.
Multiply the top number by the ones digit – the first partial product.
Write a place-holding zero, then multiply by the tens digit – the second partial product.
Add the two partial products together.
Check the answer against your estimate.

methods.title: How to do long division
steps: Estimate the answer first.
Check the how to do long division result carefully.
Check the how to do long division result carefully.

methods.title: Divide: how many times does the divisor fit into the leading digits?
steps: Multiply that digit by the divisor and write the product below.
Subtract to find the remainder so far.
Bring down the next digit and repeat the cycle until no digits remain.

methods.title: How to express a remainder
steps: Whole number: write ‘r’ and the amount left over.
Fraction: put the remainder over the divisor.
Decimal: continue dividing, or divide the remainder by the divisor.
Choose the form that fits the real-world context.

methods.title: Long division with a two-digit divisor
steps: Solution: Divide: 18 into 43 goes 2 (18 × 2 = 36), remainder 7.
Bring down the 2 to make 72.
Divide: 18 into 72 goes 4 exactly (18 × 4 = 72), remainder 0.

methods.title: A remainder in three forms
methods.example: Calculate 247 ÷ 8 and write the remainder as a whole number.
steps: Solution: 8 into 24 goes 3 exactly (write 3, remainder 0); bring down the 7.
8 into 7 goes 0 remainder 7.
So 247 ÷ 8 = 30 remainder 7.
As a fraction this is 30 and 7/8; as a decimal, 7 ÷ 8 = 0.875, so 30.875.

workedExamples.title: Short multiplication
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Estimate: 274 rounds to 270, and 270 × 6 = 1620. Now work through the columns. Ones: 4 × 6 = 24, write 4, carry 2. Tens: 7 × 6 = 42, plus the carried 2 = 44, write 4, carry 4. Hundreds: 2 × 6 = 12, plus the carried 4 = 16. The answer is 1644, which is close to the estimate of 1620, so it is sensible.

workedExamples.title: Long multiplication
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Estimate: 70 × 24 = 1680. Multiply by the ones: 67 × 4 = 268. Multiply by the tens: 67 × 20 = 1340 (write the place-holding zero, then 67 × 2 = 134). Add the partial products: 268 + 1340 = 1608. This is close to 1680, so we are confident.

workedExamples.title: Long multiplication with a 3-digit number
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Estimate: 240 × 15 = 3600. Multiply by the ones: 238 × 5 = 1190. Multiply by the tens: 238 × 10 = 2380. Add: 1190 + 2380 = 3570. Close to 3600 – correct.

workedExamples.title: Short division
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Estimate: 156 is close to 150, and 150 ÷ 13 is about 11 to 12. Working: 13 into 15 goes 1 remainder 2 (carry the 2 to make 26); 13 into 26 goes 2 exactly. So 156 ÷ 13 = 12. Check with the inverse: 12 × 13 = 156. It matches.

workedExamples.title: Long division with a two-digit divisor
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Divide: 18 into 43 goes 2 (18 × 2 = 36), remainder 7. Bring down the 2 to make 72. Divide: 18 into 72 goes 4 exactly (18 × 4 = 72), remainder 0. So 432 ÷ 18 = 24. Check: 24 × 18 = 432.

workedExamples.title: A remainder in three forms
workedExamples.prompt: Calculate 247 ÷ 8 and write the remainder as a whole number.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 8 into 24 goes 3 exactly (write 3, remainder 0); bring down the 7. 8 into 7 goes 0 remainder 7. So 247 ÷ 8 = 30 remainder 7. As a fraction this is 30 and 7/8; as a decimal, 7 ÷ 8 = 0.875, so 30.875. Check: 30 × 8 + 7 = 240 + 7 = 247.

workedExamples.title: Deciding how to treat the remainder
workedExamples.prompt: A school trip has 234 students. Each bus holds 45 students. How many buses are needed?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 234 ÷ 45 = 5 remainder 9 (because 45 × 5 = 225, and 234 − 225 = 9). Five buses would leave 9 students behind, which is not allowed, so we round up to 6 buses. The context tells us to round up.

workedExamples.title: Multiplying and dividing by powers of ten
workedExamples.solution: Solution: For 7.5 × 1000, the digits move three places left: 7.5 becomes 7500. For 4700 ÷ 100, the digits move two places right: 4700 becomes 47. Always think of the digits shifting, not of adding or removing zeros.

workedExamples.title: Missing number and inverse operations
workedExamples.prompt: If 24 × ? = 96, what is the missing number?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Multiplication and division are inverses, so to find the missing factor we divide: 96 ÷ 24 = 4. Check by multiplying back: 24 × 4 = 96.

workedExamples.title: A two-step word problem
workedExamples.prompt: A cinema in Nairobi has 15 rows with 28 seats in each row. If 80% of the seats are filled, how many people are watching?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: First multiply to find the total seats: 15 × 28 = 420. Then find 80% of 420. Since 10% of 420 is 42, 80% is 42 × 8 = 336. So 336 people are watching. This problem needed multiplication twice – a good example of a multi-step calculation.

workedExamples.title: Find all the factor pairs of 36
workedExamples.prompt: Find all the factor pairs of 36.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: A number multiplied by 8 gives the same result as adding 84 to the number. What is the number?
workedExamples.solution: 1) Let the number be n. 8n = n + 84, so 7n = 84, n = 12. Check: 8 × 12 = 96 and 12 + 84 = 96.

practice.prompt: A number multiplied by 8 gives the same result as adding 84 to the number. What is the number?
practice.answer: 1) Let the number be n. 8n = n + 84, so 7n = 84, n = 12. Check: 8 × 12 = 96 and 12 + 84 = 96.
practice.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

activities.title: Warehouse Manager Challenge
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a calculator (for checking only).
steps: Become a warehouse manager in Berbera and solve these multiplication problems. Estimate first, then calculate, then check that your answer makes sense.
Soup delivery: Each tray holds 24 cans and each box holds 6 trays. You receive 8 boxes of tomato soup. How many cans is that? Then work out 5 boxes of a second kind and find the grand total.
Box loading: Each box has a mass of 18 kg. Truck A carries 45 boxes and Truck B carries 37 boxes. Find the mass on each truck and how much more Truck A carries.
Inventory count: The warehouse has 12 aisles, each with 28 shelves, each holding 15 boxes. How many boxes can it hold? If it is 80% full, how many boxes are stored?
Challenge: Design your own warehouse scenario and write a multiplication problem for a family member to solve.

activities.title: Division Detective
steps: Solve each mystery by choosing the right operation. Estimate first, then calculate, and write your answer as a full sentence.
A music group earns 432 shillings, shared equally so that each member gets 54. How many people are in the group?
A librarian has 756 books and each shelf holds 28. How many shelves are needed? Will any shelf be partly filled?
A trip has 234 students and each bus holds 45. How many buses are needed? Explain how you treated the remainder.
A bakery makes 648 sambusas and packs them in boxes of 24. How many complete boxes, and how many are left over?

activities.title: Powers-of-Ten Pattern Explorer
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and coloured pens.
steps: Discover the patterns that make multiplying and dividing by 10, 100 and 1000 so fast.
Complete: 7 × 10, 7 × 100, 7 × 1000. Then try 23 × 10, 23 × 100, 23 × 1000. What pattern do you notice?
Complete: 470 ÷ 10, 4700 ÷ 100, 47000 ÷ 1000. How is this the reverse of multiplying?
Now try decimals: 3.6 × 10, 3.6 × 100, 3.6 ÷ 10, 3.6 ÷ 100. Use colour to show which way the digits move.
Write a sentence explaining, in your own words, why ‘add a zero’ does not work for decimals.

activities.title: Divisibility Rules Game
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a timer (optional).
steps: Become an expert at quickly testing whether numbers are divisible by 2, 4, 5 and 8.
For each number – 144, 275, 368, 1000, 2568, 3125, 4096 – write Y or N for divisibility by 2, 4, 5 and 8.
Make the smallest 4-digit number divisible by both 4 and 5.
Make the largest 3-digit number divisible by 8.
Speed challenge: Time yourself testing 1248, 2367, 3124, 4892 and 5736 for divisibility by 4.

activities.title: Multiplication Grid Art (Investigation)
activities.materials: squared paper, a ruler, and coloured pencils.
steps: Explore patterns hidden inside the multiplication table.
Draw a 10 by 10 grid. In each square write the product of its row number and column number.
Colour every square with an even product one colour and every odd product another colour.
Describe the pattern you see. Why do two odd numbers multiply to give an odd product, but any even factor makes the product even?
Try a second grid where you colour only the multiples of 3. What shape does the pattern make?

activities.title: Family Market Maths (Group Task)
activities.materials: your family or friends, and some small objects as pretend goods and shillings.
steps: Set up a pretend market stall with prices, for example mangoes at 15 shillings each.
Take turns buying several items and using multiplication to find the total cost.
Give a large ‘note’ and use division or subtraction to work out the change.
Share a basket of ‘dates’ equally among everyone playing and record the remainder. Talk about what to do with the leftovers.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand multiplication and division best? What real-world uses did you discover? Which method do you now feel most confident with, and which needs more practice? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: For whole numbers this looks like adding
rules.text: For whole numbers this looks like adding or removing zeros. For example, 47 × 100 = 4700 (two zeros added) and 4700 ÷ 100 = 47 (two zeros removed). But be careful: the true rule is about digits moving places, not simply ‘adding zeros’. The ‘add a zero’ shortcut fails as soon as decimals appear, so it is far safer to think of the digits shifting.

terms: A number that divides exactly into another
The result of multiplying by a whole number
The amount left over after dividing
Two factors / more than two factors
The operation that undoes another (× and ÷)
commonMistakes: Write 0 first when multiplying by the tens digit
Move the digits one place left instead
Round up, round down, or write a fraction/decimal

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Two numbers have a product of 144 and a sum of 30. What are the two numbers?
Find the smallest number divisible by 2, 4, 5 and 8.

reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 40 – the least common multiple of 2, 4, 5 and 8.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Store A sells apples in bags of 12 for 8 shillings. Store B sells bags of 18 for 11 shillings. Which is better value per apple?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1234321 (the pattern reads up to the number of ones then back down).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Multiplying and Dividing by 10, 100 and 1000.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: There is a beautiful pattern for multiplying and dividing by powers of ten, and it works because of place value. When you multiply by 10, every digit becomes worth ten times as much, so each digit moves one place to the left. When you multiply by 100, digits move two places left; by 1000, three places left. Dividing is the exact reverse: digits move to the right – one place for 10, two places for 100, three places for 1000. For whole numbers this looks like adding or removing zeros. For example, 47 × 100 = 4700…

questions.question: Calculate 274 × 6.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 11 methods.
questions.explanation: Estimate: 274 rounds to 270, and 270 × 6 = 1620. Now work through the columns. Ones: 4 × 6 = 24, write 4, carry 2. Tens: 7 × 6 = 42, plus the carried 2 = 44, write 4, carry 4. Hundreds: 2 × 6 = 12, plus the carried 4 = 16. The answer is…

questions.question: Calculate 67 × 24.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 11 methods.
questions.explanation: Estimate: 70 × 24 = 1680. Multiply by the ones: 67 × 4 = 268. Multiply by the tens: 67 × 20 = 1340 (write the place-holding zero, then 67 × 2 = 134). Add the partial products: 268 + 1340 = 1608. This is close to 1680, so we are confident.

questions.question: Calculate 238 × 15.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 11 methods.
questions.explanation: Estimate: 240 × 15 = 3600. Multiply by the ones: 238 × 5 = 1190. Multiply by the tens: 238 × 10 = 2380. Add: 1190 + 2380 = 3570. Close to 3600 – correct.

questions.question: Calculate 156 ÷ 13 using division, then check.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 11 methods.
questions.explanation: Estimate: 156 is close to 150, and 150 ÷ 13 is about 11 to 12. Working: 13 into 15 goes 1 remainder 2 (carry the 2 to make 26); 13 into 26 goes 2 exactly. So 156 ÷ 13 = 12. Check with the inverse: 12 × 13 = 156. It matches.

questions.question: Calculate 432 ÷ 18.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 11 methods.
questions.explanation: Divide: 18 into 43 goes 2 (18 × 2 = 36), remainder 7. Bring down the 2 to make 72. Divide: 18 into 72 goes 4 exactly (18 × 4 = 72), remainder 0. So 432 ÷ 18 = 24. Check: 24 × 18 = 432.

questions.question: What does “Product” mean?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 11 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Product means The answer to a multiplication.

questions.question: What does “Quotient” mean?
options: A number that divides exactly into another

questions.question: What does “Dividend” mean?
options: A number that divides exactly into another
The result of multiplying by a whole number
questions.hint: Use the Unit 11 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Dividend means The number being divided.

games.title: Quick Match: Product
games.description: Practise product through four short challenges.

rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 11 methods.
rounds.explanation: Estimate: 274 rounds to 270, and 270 × 6 = 1620. Now work through the columns. Ones: 4 × 6 = 24, write 4, carry 2. Tens: 7 × 6 = 42, plus the carried 2 = 44, write 4, carry 4. Hundreds: 2 × 6 = 12, plus the carried 4 = 16. The answer is…

rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 11 methods.
rounds.explanation: Estimate: 156 is close to 150, and 150 ÷ 13 is about 11 to 12. Working: 13 into 15 goes 1 remainder 2 (carry the 2 to make 26); 13 into 26 goes 2 exactly. So 156 ÷ 13 = 12. Check with the inverse: 12 × 13 = 156. It matches.

rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

games.title: Concept Quest: Quotient
games.description: Practise quotient through four short challenges.

rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 11 methods.
rounds.explanation: Estimate: 70 × 24 = 1680. Multiply by the ones: 67 × 4 = 268. Multiply by the tens: 67 × 20 = 1340 (write the place-holding zero, then 67 × 2 = 134). Add the partial products: 268 + 1340 = 1608. This is close to 1680, so we are confident.

rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 11 methods.
rounds.explanation: Divide: 18 into 43 goes 2 (18 × 2 = 36), remainder 7. Bring down the 2 to make 72. Divide: 18 into 72 goes 4 exactly (18 × 4 = 72), remainder 0. So 432 ÷ 18 = 24. Check: 24 × 18 = 432.

rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, copy every worked example onto paper and try it yourself, and only move on once you feel sure. Your AI tutor is available day and night to set you extra questions, explain a step in a different way, or mark your working. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels difficult. Nobody is rushing you – accuracy matters far more than speed at this stage.

I can why does this matter so much? Multiplication and division are everywhere in daily life. Shopkeepers use them to price goods and give correct change. Farmers use them to work out how many sacks of grain a harvest will fill. Families use them to share food fairly and to budget their shillings. Builders use them to count bricks and tiles. When you become fluent with these written methods, you gain a skill you will use for the rest of your life – and you build the strong foundation you need for fractions, decimals, ratio and algebra in the years ahead. Keep a pencil and paper beside you at all times, because mathematics is a subject you must do, not merely read.
I can estimate the size of an answer before you calculate, by rounding.
I can multiply a three-digit number by a one-digit number using short multiplication.
I can multiply a three-digit number by a two-digit number using long multiplication.
I can divide using short division and long division by a one-digit or two-digit number.
I can express remainders as whole numbers, fractions, or decimals, choosing sensibly for the context.
I can multiply and divide whole numbers and decimals by 10, 100 and 1000.
